segments.s127-g105: تهمتن is the proper name (subject). چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause here meaning “when.” بشنید is the simple past 3rd-person singular (narrative past) “he heard.” گفتار اوی is the object phrase “his speech/words,” where اوی is an archaic possessive/enclitic meaning “his” (attached to the noun rather than separate). The structure reads: ‘Tahmatan, when he heard his words…’.
segments.s127-g106: بسیچید is a past 3rd-person singular verb “he mustered/assembled.” و links the two actions (“and”). زی here is an archaic prepositional form meaning roughly “toward” (not the modern senses listed), introducing the goal جنگ “battle/war.” بنهاد is the past 3rd-person of نهاد with the prefix بـ- meaning “he set/placed” and together بنهاد روی (“set [his] face”) forms the idiom “turned/set his face toward” (روی = “face” as the direct object). The clause thus means “he mustered and set his face toward battle.”
segments.s127-g1: The phrase is an English-style noun phrase built from Persian elements: 'یکی' is an indefinite 'a certain', followed by the head noun 'مرد' (man). 'بیدار' is an adjective meaning 'alert/awake' modifying 'مرد'. 'جوینده راه' is a compound: 'جوینده' (active participle 'seeker') + 'راه' ('way, road'), together 'seeker of the road' (i.e. a scout). Word order here places adjectives and participial modifiers after or next to the noun, as common in Persian poetry.
segments.s127-g2: 'فرستاد' is a past‑tense 3rd‑person singular verb 'he sent / dispatched'. 'نزدیک' here functions as a prepositional/locative adverb meaning 'near / to the vicinity of' and governs the following name phrase. 'کاووس شاه' is a name + title: proper name 'Kavus' followed by 'شاه' (king). The construction means 'sent (him) near King Kavus' i.e. 'sent toward/into the presence of King Kavus.'
segments.s127-g3: 'به نزدیک' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'near / in the presence of'. 'سالار' is a title 'commander/chief' and 'هاماوران' is a place-name or descriptive noun (literally 'level plains' in origin) forming the full title 'commander of Hamavaran'. The phrase places the action/location 'near the commander of Hamavaran.'
segments.s127-g4: 'بشد' is the classical past/experiential form meaning 'it happened / he became' (here best read 'he became'). 'نامداری' is a predicative noun 'renown, fame' that follows the verb. 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from / because of / among', and 'کندآوران' is a plural/collective form built from 'کندآور' (literally 'those who slow' or 'slow‑makers'); in this line it functions as the source or group relation ('from/among the کندآوران'). Together: 'he became renowned (نامداری بشد) among/from the کندآوران.' (Note: 'کندآوران' is somewhat obscure/poetic in meaning and may designate a group or characteristic—see senses mapping.)
segments.s127-g5: 'یکی' = 'a certain'; 'نامه' = 'letter' (direct object). 'بنوشت' is a classical past form 'he wrote' (the supplied lemma list did not include the writing sense, so the semantic index is not available). 'با' is the preposition 'with' introducing an accompanying set/contents, and 'گیر و دار' is a fixed expression meaning 'snags, entanglements, trappings, or bustle' (literally 'catch and hold') used here to indicate various trappings or turmoil that accompany the letter.
segments.s127-g6: 'پر از' is the common construction 'full of' (پر = 'full', plus preposition 'از' = 'of'). It lists the contents of that fullness: 'گرز' (mace/club) and 'شمشیر' (sword), joined by 'و' (and). 'پرکارزار' is an adjectival/nominal compound meaning 'war‑ridden' or 'warlike' and further describes the letter's martial contents. The whole phrase means the letter was laden with weapons and warlike matter.
segments.s127-g7: 'که' introduces a relative/that‑clause here meaning 'who/that'. 'بر' is used as 'against' (classical Persian often uses بر for 'against, onto'), 'شاه ایران' = 'the Shah/king of Iran' (noun + genitive/place), 'کمین' = 'ambush', and 'ساختی' is 2nd‑person past 'you made / you set up'. Grammatically this is a clause accusing/descrbing someone: 'who set an ambush against the Shah of Iran.'
segments.s127-g8: 'بپیوستن' is an infinitive meaning 'to join' or 'upon joining'; 'اندر' = 'in/into', 'بد' = 'bad / misfortune' (here used as a noun 'misfortune, harm'), and 'انداختی' is 2nd‑person past 'you threw / you cast'. The verb comes at the end (classical Persian order) and the phrase as a whole means 'you cast into misfortune upon joining' or 'you caused (them) to be thrown into evil when they joined.' Note that 'انداختی' can be understood causatively ('caused to be put'), which fits this context.
segments.s127-g73: همه دل پر از بیم برخاستند is a poetic inversion: همه دل (literally “all heart(s)”) = “everyone’s hearts,” followed by the adjectival phrase پر از بیم (“full of fear”). The verb برخاستند is 3rd-person plural past (“they rose/rose up”) and here predicates the collective reaction (hearts rising in fear).
segments.s127-g74: سپاهی ز کشور بیاراستند: سپاهی = “an army/host”; the preposition ز (poetic for از) means “from,” so ز کشور = “from the country/realm.” بیاراستند is a past 3rd-plural transitive verb meaning here “they arrayed/mustered” (classical/poetic use). The agent is implicit (‘they’—the rulers/people).
segments.s127-g75: نهادند سر سوی هاماوران: نهادند is past 3rd-plural of نهادن “to place/set”; here it forms the idiom نهادنِ سر سوی X = “to turn/set (one’s) head toward X,” i.e., to direct themselves toward. سر is the direct object (“head,” used idiomatically for direction or outset), سوی = “toward,” and هاماوران = “the even/level plains.”
segments.s127-g76: زمین کوه گشت از کران تا کران is a compact predicative clause: گشت is past of گشتن “to become/turn,” so زمین کوه گشت = “the ground became (like) mountains” (poetic word order places زمین before the predicate). از کران تا کران is a span “from edge to edge/as far as the limits,” specifying extent.
segments.s127-g77: سپه کوه تا کوه صف برکشید: سپه (subject) “the army/host,” followed by the range کوه تا کوه “from mountain to mountain.” صف is the formation/line, and برکشید (past 3rd-plural) is a poetic verb meaning “raised/drew up” (i.e., formed a line). Word order places the extent between subject and object for emphasis.
segments.s127-g78: پی مور شد بر زمین ناپدید is idiomatic and compressed: پی مور شد is a classical poetic simile meaning “became like ants in succession/swarms” (پی here introduces the simile; مور = ant; شد = became). بر + زمین = “on/upon the ground,” and ناپدید = “vanished.” The whole describes them thinning into antlike specks and disappearing on the plain.
segments.s127-g79: چو رستم چنان دید نزدیک شاه: چو is the conjunction “when”; رستم is the subject; چنان “thus/so” modifies the verb دید (past “saw”). نزدیک شاه is a prepositional/adjectival phrase “near the king” (نزدیک before شاه as is common in Persian), indicating location/relationship of what he saw.
segments.s127-g80: نهانی برافگند مردی به راه: نهانی is an adverb “secretly.” برافگند is a past 3rd‑person form used in older/poetic Persian meaning roughly “sent forth / dispatched” (literally “threw forth” in the lexical list but here idiomatically “sent”). مردی = “a man,” and به راه = “onto the road / along the way.” The verb governs the phrase and places the action at the end (typical Persian order).
segments.s127-g17: فرستاده شد is a passive construction: فرستاده (past participle 'sent') + شد (auxiliary 'was'), so 'was sent'. نزد + place/person expresses direction toward or 'into the presence of' (here: to Hamavaran). هاماوران is a toponym/landscape noun 'the plains' used as the destination.
segments.s127-g18: This clause contains the verb phrase that follows the passive sending: پیام یکایک سران is the object 'the message of/for each of the chiefs'. بدادش appears here in the text but the provided sense list does not include the verbal sense 'gave/delivered'; because the listed senses don't match the usage, its sense index is set to -1. The word order puts پیغام (message) before its specifier یکایک سران ('each of the leaders').
segments.s127-g19: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'. پیغام بشنید and نامه بخواند are two past narrative actions joined by و: بشنید is simple past 'he heard' and بخواند here is past narrative 'he read/recited (the letter)'. The verbs are in the classical narrative past and follow the subordinate چو.
segments.s127-g20: ز here functions as a causal/relational preposition 'because of / at' (poetic form of از). کردار خود = 'his own deeds' (کردار = deed; خود = 'own'). در شگفتی بماند is a stative result: در + شگفتی 'in astonishment' + بماند (present/subjunctive form used in narrative sense here 'remained/was left').
segments.s127-g21: چو again marks 'when'. برخواند نامه is the verb 'he read aloud/recited the letter' (برخواند = read/recite). سرش is 'his head' (سر + possessive ش), and خیره شد is a compound stative 'became dazed/stunned' (خیره = dazed/astonished; شد = became). Word order is Subject (implied) + verb phrase + result clause.
segments.s127-g22: جهان پیش چشمش همه تیره شد is a descriptive result clause: جهان 'the world' + پیش چشمش 'before his eyes' (prepositional phrase), همه 'all/entirely' modifies تیره شد (took on darkness). شد is the past/resultative 'became'.
segments.s127-g23: چنین داد پاسخ is 'he gave this reply' (چنین = thus; داد = gave; پاسخ = answer). که introduces the reported content/quote 'that/that which'. کاووس کی = 'when will Kāvus...' (کی used interrogatively as 'when'). The clause sets up the content of the answer.
segments.s127-g24: به هامون = 'to the plain' (به + place). دگر نسپرد is a negative/limiting idea 'no longer (shall) send' — دگر here indicates 'again/anymore' and نسپرد (from سپردن) is used in sense of 'send/commit/dispatch'. نیز پی combines دو particles: نیز 'also/ nor (in negative contexts)' and پی 'after/pursuit' (here 'pursue after'), so the clause questions both sending and pursuit.
segments.s127-g25: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you' (topic). هرگه که آیی is a temporal/subordinate clause 'whenever (هرگه) you come (آیی)'; آیی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive/optative of آمدن used here for a general/conditional time clause. به بربرستان is a prepositional phrase showing destination ('to/into the barbarian land'); word order places the time clause before the destination phrase for poetic emphasis.
segments.s127-g26: نبینی is a negated present/subjunctive-form meaning 'you do not see' / 'you see not' (used here generically). مگر is the exception particle 'except' (hence 'see nothing except…'). تیغ و گرز گران is the noun phrase 'sword and heavy mace(s)', with the adjective گران following the nouns (typical Persian order: noun + adjective).
segments.s127-g27: همین here means 'this very' (pointing to the immediately preceding stanza). بند و زندانت joins two nouns 'stanza (بند) and your prison (زندان + possessive -ت)'; آراستست is the archaic/poetic form آراسته‌ست ('is adorned/arranged'), 3rd-person singular predicate applying to both coordinated nouns.
segments.s127-g28: اگر is the conditional 'if'. رایت here is the noun 'banner/standard' (not the verb 'you saw'). این آرزو is the object 'this wish/desire'. خواستست is an archaic past form meaning '(he/it) wished' or 'was desired' (3rd-person); together the clause reads 'if the banner desired this wish'.
segments.s127-g29: بیایم is the 1st-person verb form (optative/present) 'I come / I will come' expressing intention. بجنگ is the purpose infinitive 'to fight' (بیایم بجنگ = 'I will come to fight'). The sequence تو من با سپاه places the object/address (تو 'you') and stressed subject pronoun من 'I' before the instrumental phrase با سپاه 'with an army' — a somewhat inverted, emphatic word order typical of epic diction.
segments.s127-g30: برین گونه = 'in this manner/way' (بر + این/ین forming a poetic adverbial 'in this way'). سازیم is 1st-person plural 'we make/construct/establish' (present/optative meaning 'we will do'), and آیین و راه are the coordinated objects 'custom/ordinance and way/method' — 'thus we will set the custom and the course.'
segments.s127-g31: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is 3rd-person past 'he heard'. پاسخ‌گو is an agent noun 'the respondent/answerer' and پیلتن is a proper name/place; together they form the subject ('Piltan the respondent') placed after the verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s127-g32: دلیران is the plural 'the brave ones'. لشکر here links the group to the host/army (دلیرانِ لشکر = 'the army's brave men' / 'brave soldiers of the host'). شدند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they became' used idiomatically with انجمن 'assembly/meeting' as the predicate complement — i.e. 'they gathered/formed an assembly.'
segments.s127-g41: ببایست is an impersonal past-necessity verb phrase (‚it was necessary/ought to beʻ). It introduces a dependent تا-clause (until) that gives the content of what must happen: گاهش آمد به جنگ — literally ‘his time came to/for battle’. Note the order: subject/possessive گاهش (his time) precedes آمد (came) and به جنگ (for battle) marks the purpose/occasion.
segments.s127-g42: نبد is an archaic negative existential (older form of نبود) meaning ‘there was not’. It governs روزگار (time/fate) as its subject: ‘there was no … in the course of time’. سکون و درنگ are two nouns in coordination (‘stillness and delay’), giving what the time lacked — i.e. no calm or pause. Word order is poetic: negator + subject + complements.
segments.s127-g43: چو ('when') introduces a temporal clause. بیرون شد is a two-word verbal phrase meaning ‘went out’ (شد here functions as the past verbal form following بیرون). از شهر خود با سپاه is the source/means phrase: از شهر خود ‘out of his city’ and با سپاه ‘with an army/host’ attached as circumstantial information.
segments.s127-g44: This is a compressed poetic clause: به‌روز درخشان (‘in the shining day’ / lit. ‘of-day shining’) sets the temporal/background image, then شب آمد سیاه (‘black night came’) states the event. Note the inverse order and adjective placement: درخشان qualifies به‌روز and سیاه follows آمد as predicate adjective describing the night that came.
segments.s127-g45: چپ و راست is an adverbial phrase (‘left and right’ / ‘to the left and right’) that sets the disposition; لشکر is the noun 'army/host' and بیاراستند is the finite past verb ‘they arrayed/ordered’ placed at the end (typical Persian SOV order in older/poetic style).
segments.s127-g46: به جنگ اندرون is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘to/for battle within’ (اندرون = inside/within). نامور is an adjective modifying the battle (‘renowned’). خواستند is the past tense of خواستن used here as ‘they sought/desired/endeavoured’, completing the clause: they arranged the host and (then) sought the famed inner battle.
segments.s127-g47: گو at the start marks reported speech/introduces what follows (‘then/said’), followed by the name پیلتن and گفت (here functioning to mark that he spoke). جنگی منم is a predicative clause: جنگی (‘warlike/warrior’) + منم (‘I am’). The element order is emphatic/poetic (predicate before copula-like منم).
segments.s127-g48: به آوردگه is a locative/benefactive phrase ‘at the place of meeting/venue’ (آوردگه = place of encounter). بر here is a prepositional particle that in context links to درنگی (‘delay/hesitation’) — the phrase بر درنگی منم is best read as a concise boast: ‘I stand (as) against delay’ or ‘I am not one to tarry’ (literally, ‘on/with respect to delay, I am [so]’). منم closes the clause as the copular ‘I am’. The phrasing is compact and characteristic of heroic speech.
segments.s127-g33: سوی راه دریا is a prepositional/directional phrase ('toward the sea‑road') placed before the verb; بیامد is a simple past ('they came/they moved'); به جنگ after the verb expresses purpose ('for battle' or 'to fight'). Word order (direction → verb → purpose) is typical of narrative Persian.
segments.s127-g34: که introduces a causal clause ('for/because'); بر خشک = 'on/over the dry (land)' (بر + adjective), and ره is the subject 'the road/route'. بود is the past copula 'was', while با درنگ is an adverbial phrase 'with delay/slowly'. The clause shows poetic inversion: the copula precedes the subject for rhythm ('...بود ره با درنگ' = 'the road was with delay').
segments.s127-g35: به کشتی و زورق is a directional/means phrase ('onto ships and skiffs'); سپاهی گران is a noun phrase where the adjective گران follows the noun (poetic/older Persian order) meaning 'a heavy/mighty army'. There is no verb in this segment — it sets the scene/means.
segments.s127-g36: بشد is an archaic/simple past form used here to mean 'they went/reached' or 'it came to be that they were'; تا introduces the extent 'as far as', and سر مرز هاماوران is the locative phrase 'the head/frontier of Hamâvaran'. The verb plus تا+place expresses motion reaching that limit.
segments.s127-g37: به + nouns (تاراج و کشتن) plus the verb نهادند is a common construction meaning 'they set themselves to / began [pillage and killing]'; here به marks what they put themselves to, and نهادند is the past transitive 'they placed/commenced'. روی after the verb functions as a prepositional/adverbial element ('upon/against'), linking to what is acted on.
segments.s127-g38: ز خون is a prepositional phrase 'from/by blood'; روی کشور = 'the surface/face of the country' (روی here = surface), شده is the resultative 'became/has become', and the reduplicated جوی جوی ('stream stream') is a poetic intensifier meaning 'streams upon streams' or 'a running of streams'. Word order places the source (ز خون) before the subject (روی کشور) for emphasis.
segments.s127-g39: خبر شد is an impersonal/patient construction 'news happened/arrived' meaning 'news reached'; به شاه هماور marks the indirect object ('to King Homâvar'); ازین ('of this/about this') introduces the reported content. The verb شد is simple past/intransitive 'occurred/was conveyed'.
segments.s127-g40: که introduces the reported clause 'that ...'; نهادست is an older/perfective third‑person form meaning 'he placed/has put' (here 'had placed'); بر رخش زین literally 'on Rakhsh (put) the saddle' — Persian word order is flexible in poetry, so the direct object زین ('saddle') appears after the locative phrase, but the literal sense is 'placed the saddle on Rakhsh'.
segments.s127-g49: برآورد is a past tense verb (3rd sg.) meaning 'he lifted/hoisted' in this poetic context; the direct object گرز گران را (the heavy mace) is marked by the object marker را and appears before the locative phrase به دوش ('onto/onto his shoulder'). به + دوش gives the placement 'upon the shoulder' (دوش = 'shoulder'). Word order (object before verb) and the particle را follow classical Persian/poetic patterns.
segments.s127-g50: برانگیخت is a past tense (3rd sg.) transitive verb 'he roused/raised', here taking رخش (the war-steed) as its object; و links the next clause. In the second clause برآمد خروش the verb برآمد ('arose/issued forth') precedes its noun خروش ('a cry/roar'), a common poetic inversion meaning 'a cry arose'.
segments.s127-g51: چو دیدند = 'when they saw' (چو = 'when'); دیدند is 3rd pl. past 'they saw'. لشکر is 'the host/army' and is the thing perceived here; بر و یال اوی is a locative/possessive phrase: بر 'on/upon' + یال 'mane' with اوی 'his' (genitive). So the clause says 'when they saw the host on his back and his mane' (i.e. the impressive appearance of horse/steed).
segments.s127-g52: به چنگ اندرون = 'into/within the grip' or 'in his clutch' (به marking direction/placement, چنگ used idiomatically as 'grip/hold', اندرون 'inside/within'). گرز و گوپال اوی are the items possessed/in his grasp (گرز 'mace', گوپال here is used as a named object or proper name for a weapon/thing), and اوی is the possessive 'his'. The clause thus lists what was in his hand.
segments.s127-g53: تو گفتی که introduces reported speech ('you said that'). In the following clause دلشان برآمد ز تن is an idiom: دلشان 'their hearts' (دل + شان 'their') and برآمد (past 'rose/left') ز تن 'from the body' — i.e. 'their hearts left their bodies' meaning they were stricken/overcome with fear. Note the use of که to introduce the reported clause.
segments.s127-g54: ز هولش = 'because of/from his/its terror' (ز = 'from/because of', هولش = 'his/its terror'); پراگنده شد انجمن is 'the assembly was scattered' (پراگنده 'scattered', شد as the past/copular 'became/was', انجمن 'assembly/gathering'). The verb precedes the noun in poetic order but reads naturally as 'the assembly became scattered because of the terror'.
segments.s127-g55: همان شاه = 'that same king' (همان as an emphatic/demonstrative). با نامور سرکشان = 'with his famed/renowned chiefs/leading men' (با = 'with', نامور = 'renowned'). سرکشان is a plural substantive referring to notable subordinates/chiefs; the phrase describes accompaniment or collective leaders with the king.
segments.s127-g56: ز رستم چو دیدند یک‌یک نشان = 'when they saw one by one the signs/tokens of Rostam' (ز رستم = 'of/from Rostam', چو = 'when', دیدند = 'they saw', یک‌یک = 'one by one/each', نشان = 'token/sign/mark'). Word order is the usual poetic sequence, and this clause sets up their reaction upon seeing Rostam's signs.
segments.s127-g65: کزین = کز + این, a poetic prepositional phrase 'from/of this'; پادشاهی is the noun 'kingship/kingdom'. بذان here is the combination of preposition ب (be, 'to/onto') + an archaic demonstrative (بدان = 'to that'), so the whole clause means 'from this kingship to that is not far'. نیست (negation + copula) follows the noun phrase and دور ('far') is the predicate adjective; the word order is typical for older/poetic Persian where the prepositional cluster precedes the predicate.
segments.s127-g66: بهم بود is a small clause meaning 'were together' (بهم = together/with one another, بود = was/were). The following series نیک و بد و جنگ و سور lists paired/contrasting states or events ('good and bad, war and feast') that are presented as co‑occurring; the conjunction و simply links the nouns in a poetic catalog.
segments.s127-g67: گرایدونک is an archaic/poetic compound meaning roughly 'if you utter a small cry/complaint' (گر- marks the conditional 'if' and the root gives the small lament). باشید is the polite/imperative/subjunctive form 'be (you)' here functioning as a command or exhortation 'be (one) with me'. با من یکی literally 'be one with me' (با = with; من = me; یکی = one/united).
segments.s127-g68: ز رستم نترسم is 'I do not fear Rostam' where ز (= از, poetic variant) introduces the object of fear, رستم is the person, and نترسم is the 1st person present negative of ترسیدن 'to fear' ('I do not fear'). به جنگ اندکی attaches a local/temporal phrase 'in/for a little battle' (به = in/for; جنگ = battle; اندکی = a little), qualifying the extent of the speaker's confidence.
segments.s127-g69: وگرنه is the conjunctive 'otherwise'. بدان here is again the cluster ب + دان (be + that) pointing to 'that (kingship)'—so وگرنه بدان پادشاهی رسد = 'otherwise that kingship will come/attain (someone)'. رسد is the present (narrative/future) form of رسیدن 'to reach/attain', used here with future/inevitable sense.
segments.s127-g70: درازست is a predicative adjective 'is/was long/extended' describing the extent of reach. بر هر سویی is a prepositional phrase 'upon every side' (بر = on/upon; هر = every; سویی = side/direction). دست بد places the noun دست ('hand', here idiomatically 'power' or 'control') before the adjective بد ('bad/evil') — a poetic order meaning 'an evil hand' or 'evil power'.
segments.s127-g71: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نامه is 'letter'; به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/at near (them)' (به = to/at; نزدیک = near/close); ایشان is the object pronoun 'them'; رسید is the past/ perfect 'reached/arrived' — together: 'when the letter reached them'.
segments.s127-g72: که introduces a reported content clause 'that ...'. رستم is the subject; بدین is be + دین (poetic 'to this') indicating motion toward 'this plain' (بدین دشت = 'onto/into this plain'); لشکر کشید is the verbal phrase 'drew/dragged/ marshaled an army' — کشیدن لشکر is the idiom 'to march/bring an army'. The whole clause reports that Rostam moved his host onto the plain.
segments.s127-g9: نه ... بود is a negative existential: 'there was no ...'. چاره جستن is an infinitival noun phrase 'to seek a remedy/way' that completes 'بود' (i.e. 'there was no man to seek a way'). به جنگ here is a prepositional phrase ('to/into war') that syntactically links to the following infinitive (it specifies the domain of the sought 'chare'). Word order places the verb 'بود' early (typical of Ferdowsi) and leaves the infinitive phrase open to the next segment.
segments.s127-g10: نرفتن is the negative infinitive/gerund 'not going' (ن- prefix + رفتن) functioning as the complement of 'چاره جستن' ('a way of not going'). به رسم is an adverbial pattern 'in the manner/of the custom' (به used adverbially). دلاور پلنگ is a poetic epithet, adjective before noun: 'the valiant/brave leopard' (پلنگ used figuratively as a warrior-epithet).
segments.s127-g11: که is the relative/connecting particle 'who/that' referring back to 'man'. در جنگ is locative 'in battle'. هرگز + نسازد is a strong negation: هرگز 'never' and نسازد (ن‑ + سازد) is the negative present/subjunctive form of ساختن 'to make/set' — here 'would not set/make (an ambush)'. کمین is the object 'an ambush' and follows the verb in the poetic word order.
segments.s127-g12: اگر چند is an idiomatic conditional 'even if/though' (اگر = if; چند gives the concessive sense). باشد is the subjunctive/present-form of 'to be' used after this conditional ('were/should be'). دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش = his). پر ز کین is a frozen adjectival phrase 'full of hatred/vengeance' with پر 'full' and ز (poetic) 'of/from' + کین 'vengeance'.
segments.s127-g13: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. شاه کاووس is the subject 'King Kavus'. یابد is the present/subjunctive form of یافتن 'to find' ('finds' or 'should find'). رها here is a participial/adjectival complement 'free/freed' functioning as object-complement ('find [you] freed') — the object (you) is supplied contextually in the next segment.
segments.s127-g14: تو is the explicit 2nd‑person subject 'you'. رستی (from رَسْتن/رَستَن family) here means 'you are saved / you will be delivered' — a result clause following the condition. ز marks origin/separation 'from', and چنگ و دم اژدها are two coordinated parts of the dragon that are being escaped: چنگ 'claw/talon' and دم 'breath' (here 'dragon's breath'); اژدها postmodifies both as 'of the dragon'.
segments.s127-g15: وگرنه 'otherwise'. بیارای is an archaic/poetic imperative/infinitival form from آراییدن / آرستن meaning 'array/prepare' (addressed to the addressee) — here 'prepare/arm'. جنگ مرا = 'my war/battle' (مرا is the possessive/object element; word segmentation in the dataset is irregular but syntactically it's 'جنگِ من'). را appears as the definite/object marker attached after the object in prose but in poetry placement can vary.
segments.s127-g16: به گردن بپیمای is an idiom: به گردن 'upon (your) neck' = 'upon your responsibility' and بپیمای is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'undertake/assume' (imperative/subjunctive sense). هنگ مرا 'my host/army' is the object being entrusted; را at the end is the object marker. The whole clause therefore commands: 'assume my host upon your neck' i.e. take command/ responsibility of my troops.
segments.s127-g97: و دیگر (and another) introduces an additional point; که is the relative/introducing particle 'who/that' linking the clause that follows; دارنده is a deverbal noun 'holder/possessor' and is the head of the relative clause; یار is the possessed noun 'friend/companion'; منست here is an archaic copular form (in effect 'is mine' / 'belongs to me' or roughly 'is for me') whose literal senses in the supplied list do not match the usage — syntactically the clause reads 'the holder of a friend (is) ...' and ties into the following phrase describing what that holder means to the speaker.
segments.s127-g98: This is a coordinated nominal clause: بزرگی 'greatness' is presented as the predicate/stock of value, و links the next coordinated phrase, مهرش 'his love' (مهر + enclitic -ش = 'his/its love/affection'), حصار 'bulwark/fortress' functions as a predicative noun, and the final منست again plays the role of the copula 'is (for) me' or 'is mine' (archaic form).
segments.s127-g99: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'; رخش درخشنده را is the definite object (رخش, the horse's name/steed, modified by درخشنده 'shining') with را marking it as definite/direct object; ده عنان is an imperative construction: ده (imperative 'give') + عنان 'the reins' — 'give the reins' (i.e. take hold of / take control of the horse).
segments.s127-g100: بیارای here is verbal (imperative or jussive) commanding an action toward the horse; گوشش is 'its ear' (گوش + enclitic ش = 'his/its ear'); به marks direction 'to/toward', نوک 'tip' and سنان 'spear' form the phrase 'to the spear's tip'. Syntactically the clause commands positioning/alerting the horse's ear toward the spear‑point.
segments.s127-g101: ازیشان (از + ایشان) introduces the group 'of them / from them'; یکی 'one' here is an indefinite 'a single one' or 'any one'; زنده 'alive' modifies that one; اندر جهان 'in the world' gives the locus. Together the phrase expresses 'not even one of them alive in the world' (the verb that negates is in the next segment).
segments.s127-g102: ممان is an archaic/verbal form meaning roughly 'I do not leave / I will not let remain' or 'I will not spare' (here negating the existence of any survivor); آشکارا 'openly' begins the adverbial pair, نه ... نه is the correlative 'neither ... nor', اندر نهان 'in secret' completes it. Grammatically the verb m-man takes the preceding nominal phrase as its object: 'I will not leave one of them alive, neither openly nor secretly.'
segments.s127-g103: فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger'; پاسخ 'an answer' is the object; بیاورد is the verb 'brings/brings back' (here rendered as past/quick action or jussive depending on context) and زود 'quickly/soon' adverbially qualifies the action. The clause reports that an answer was (or should be) brought promptly by the messenger.
segments.s127-g104: بر marks the effect 'upon' or 'on' Rostam Zāl (رستم زال as the affected person); زر 'gold' here is used metonymically (likely the golden colour of hair) and شد 'became' is the verb of change; چو 'like' introduces the simile دود 'smoke'. The clause thus says that something golden (e.g. his hair/colour) became like smoke upon Rostam Zāl—i.e. turned ashy or gray.
segments.s127-g89: فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger' (noun). بشنید is a 3rd-person past tense 'he heard'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and', linking two verbs. آمد is the past 'came' and دوان is an adverbial participle/masdari sense 'running, rushing' modifying آمد, so together 'came running'.
segments.s127-g90: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/towards the near side of' (به + نزدیک) and takes the place phrase that follows. کاووس is the proper name 'Kavus'. کی is the relative/pronominal 'who' referring back to Kavus, and شد نهان is a verb phrase 'became hidden / went into hiding' (شد = became, نهان = hidden); the relative clause (کی شد نهان) is postposed to the name.
segments.s127-g91: پیام تهمتن is a genitive phrase 'the message of Tahmatan' (پیام + name). همه is the object/affected group 'everyone/all'. باز راند is a compound meaning 'drove back' (راند = drove/expelled; باز here functions adverbially as 'back'/'again' in the sense of reversal), so the whole clause means 'Tahmatan's message drove everyone back.'
segments.s127-g92: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. The verb بشنید appears before the subject (inverted order) giving 'when (he) heard', with کاووس as the subject 'Kavus'. خیره is a predicative adjective 'staring/astonished', and بماند (past subj./past-tense sense here) means 'remained' — together 'Kavus remained stunned.'
segments.s127-g93: چنین داد پاسخ is 'thus he gave (a) reply' (چنین = thus, داد = gave, پاسخ = answer). که introduces the content of the reply ('that'). مندیش is an old/poetic form here referring to 'his/one's thought/mind' or mental state; it does not match the single gloss given and is best treated as an archaic substantive. ازین is the fused phrase 'از + این' meaning 'from this' or 'than this', marking the limiting point for گسترده that follows.
segments.s127-g94: نه precedes گسترده and negates it: 'not wide/expansive'. از بهر من is a dative/benefactive phrase 'for my sake / for me' (از بهر = for). شد is the past 'became' and زمین 'the earth' is the subject — poetic inversion places the verb before the subject: literally 'not wide, for me became the earth' → 'the earth did not seem wide for me' (i.e. the world seemed too small).
segments.s127-g95: چنین بود 'it was thus' is a fixed opening. تا بود introduces a temporal limit 'as long as / until there was' and is followed by گردان سپهر, where گردان is an adjective/participle 'revolving' modifying سپهر 'sky/heavens' — 'as long as the revolving heavens existed.' The doubled بود is a poetic reiteration of existence.
segments.s127-g96: که introduces the explanatory clause. The phrase با نوش زهرست is elliptical: با + نوش 'with drink/delight' and زهرست is the contracted copula phrase 'زهر است' = 'is poison' (here written as one word), so 'with drink there is poison.' The second half با جنگ مهر parallels the first (با جنگ 'with war') and supplies مهر 'love/affection'; the copula is omitted by ellipsis (poetic).
segments.s127-g81: که is a conjunction introducing a reported/connected clause ('that' / 'when'). شاه سه کشور here functions as a coordinated subject ('the king and the three realms' — classical Persian often omits an explicit و between coordinated nouns). برآراستند is a past third‑person plural verb (poetic/archaic stem برآراستن) meaning 'to array/prepare / put in order' and agrees with the plural subject.
segments.s127-g82: بر این‌گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner / thus' (بر used idiomatically with this expression). از جای برخاستند is a verbal phrase 'they rose up from their places' — برخاستند is past third‑person plural 'they rose up / stood up', with z‑phrase 'از جای' marking motion away 'from (their) place(s)'.
segments.s127-g83: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. جنگ را is the direct object 'the fight / battle' (را marks definiteness). من بجنبم is first‑person present/subjunctive 'I move / I stir' (بجنبم expresses the act of moving or changing position). ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from (my) place/position' and completes the idea 'if I move/stir from my place in the battle'.
segments.s127-g84: ندانند is third‑person plural negative present 'they do not / will not know' (classical usage can express what will happen). سر را is the definite object 'the head' (را marks the object). بدین کین here links the clause to 'this grudge' — idiomatically 'because of / on account of this enmity' (ب‑ + دین/kind of idiomatic construction conveying 'by/with this [grudge]'). ز پای completes the idiom 'سر را ... ز پای' meaning 'to distinguish/know the head from the foot' — together the phrase says they will not know head from foot because of this grudge.
segments.s127-g85: نباید is an impersonal modal 'one must not / it must not be' (prohibitive). کزین = ک + زین 'from this / out of this'. کین 'grudge, vengeance' is the noun head of the prepositional phrase. به تو is the indirect object 'to you / upon you'. بد رسد is a light impersonal perfect/present 'ill will befall / something bad happens' (رسد used in the sense 'to befall, to happen'), so the clause means essentially 'this grudge must not cause you misfortune.'
segments.s127-g86: که here introduces an explanatory clause 'for / because'. کار بد 'an evil deed' (کار = deed/affair; بد modifies it). از مردم 'from people / from the folk' gives the source, and بد رسد repeats the idiom 'bad befalls' (i.e. 'evil deeds originate with bad people').
segments.s127-g87: مرا is the benefactive/possessive 'for me / to me'. تخت 'throne' is the noun; بربر here is an attributive noun 'barbarian / enemy' (so تخت بربر can be understood as 'the throne vis‑à‑vis the barbarians' or more generally 'this throne' in context). نیاید (poetic negative) + به کار = 'will not be of use / will not avail' (نیاید به کار is an idiom meaning 'prove useless'). The whole means 'my throne will not help me.'
segments.s127-g88: اگر introduces the condition 'if'. بد رسد uses رسد in the sense 'to happen / befall' (here 'bad happen'). بر تن = 'upon the body' (بر = on/upon; تن = body / person). شهریار = 'the king / sovereign'. So: 'if evil befalls the king's person.'
segments.s127-g57: گریزان is a participial adjective meaning “fleeing” used adverbially to qualify the action; بیامد is a simple past (3rd person) “came.” The prepositional phrase به هاماوران (به + place) follows and marks the destination (“to the level plains”), so the whole clause reads “(they) came fleeing to the plains.”
segments.s127-g58: ز پیش is a poetic locative/relative phrase meaning “from/before” or “in front of”; it introduces the reference point تهمتن (Tahmātan, here the epithet of Rostam). سپاهی گران is a noun + adjective (سپاهی “army/host”) + (گران “heavy/weighty”), with the adjective postposed as usual; together they form the subject/agent “the heavy-armed host.” The word order is typical archaic/poetic: location/reference first, then the described troop.
segments.s127-g59: چو is the temporal conjunction “when”; بنشست is the past tense 3rd-person “sat down.” The subject سالار follows the verb (a poetic inversion: verb before subject), and با رایزن is an instrumental/comitative phrase “with an adviser.”
segments.s127-g60: دو مرد جوان is a quantified noun phrase “two young men” (numeral + noun + adjective); خواست is the past tense 3rd-person “(he) wanted/asked for.” The source or addressee is marked by از انجمن (“from the assembly”), so the clause means “(he) requested two young men from the assembly.”
segments.s127-g61: بدان here is built from the prefix ب- + دان and functions as a connective/conjunctive idea (“thereupon/so”); تا introduces a purpose clause “so that” and is followed by فرستد, which in classical Persian is a present-subjunctive or jussive form meaning “may/should send.” هم اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase meaning roughly “at once/within the same time” (هم adding emphasis or simultaneity, اندر زمان = in a moment).
segments.s127-g62: به مصر و به بربر uses repeated به to mark destinations (“to Egypt and to the Berbers”). چو is the simile marker “like/as,” and باد دمان is a poetic image “a gust/wind of the hem” (باد “wind” + دمان “skirt/hem”), describing rapidity—i.e., sent swiftly as a gust.
segments.s127-g63: یکی نامه هر یک is a compact phrase meaning “one letter each” or “each one (wrote) a letter”: یکی (one/apiece) before the noun and هر یک (each one) adding emphasis. به چنگ اندرون is a locative/possessive phrase (به + چنگ + اندرون) meaning “to/against the inner grasp / to their inward hold/bosom” — i.e., they put or held the letter close to their inward breast (چنگ here used in the sense of grasp/hold).
segments.s127-g64: نوشته is a past participle “written,” modifying the preceding noun (نامه); به درد دل is an idiomatic phrase meaning “by/with the pain of the heart” (به indicates the cause or means here). از آب خون is a genitive-of-material phrase “from the water of blood” (i.e., with blood as if it were ink). The whole string describes letters written in heartache with blood.

sentences.s127-s53: When Tahmatan heard his words, he mustered (his forces) and turned his face toward battle.
sentences.s127-s1: He dispatched a certain alert man — a seeker of the road — to the vicinity of King Kavus.
sentences.s127-s2: He became renowned in the presence of the commander of Hamavaran, celebrated (or notable) among the slow‑movers.
sentences.s127-s3: A certain man wrote a letter, with all manner of trappings and complications — full of maces and swords and warlike gear.
sentences.s127-s4: That you laid an ambush against the Shah of Iran, and cast (him/them) into misfortune when joining.
sentences.s127-s37: All hearts rose, full of fear; they mustered an army from the realm.
sentences.s127-s38: They set their heads toward the level plains; the earth turned to mountains from edge to edge.
sentences.s127-s39: The army drew up in formation from mountain to mountain; they trailed like ants and vanished upon the ground.
sentences.s127-s40: When Rostam saw this so, the king near, secretly he sent a man along the road.
sentences.s127-s9: A messenger was sent to Hamavaran, and he delivered the message to each of the chiefs.
sentences.s127-s10: When he heard the message and read the letter, he remained astonished at his own deeds.
sentences.s127-s11: When he read the letter aloud, his head became dazed, and the whole world before his eyes grew dark.
sentences.s127-s12: He replied thus: 'When will Kāvus no longer send to the Plain, nor pursue after (them)?'
sentences.s127-s13: Whenever you go into the barbarian land, you see nothing except heavy swords and maces.
sentences.s127-s14: This very stanza and your prison are adorned, if your banner desired this wish.
sentences.s127-s15: I will come to fight you with an army; in this manner we will establish custom and the path.
sentences.s127-s16: When Piltan, the responder, heard (it), the brave men of the host assembled in council.
sentences.s127-s21: It was necessary to wait until his time for battle came; there was no stillness or pause in the course of events.
sentences.s127-s22: When he went out of his city with his host, the shining day waned and black night came.
sentences.s127-s23: They arrayed the host on the left and the right; they sought the renowned battle within.
sentences.s127-s24: Then Piltan said: ‘I am a warrior in battle; at the place of encounter I am (one who causes) no delay.’
sentences.s127-s17: They moved toward the sea-road for battle, because the overland route across the dry ground was slow.
sentences.s127-s18: The heavy army went aboard ships and skiffs and reached as far as the frontier of Hamâvaran.
sentences.s127-s19: They began pillage and killing; from blood the surface of the land became streams upon streams.
sentences.s127-s20: News of this reached King Homâvar, namely that Rostam had placed the saddle on Rakhsh.
sentences.s127-s25: He lifted the heavy mace onto his shoulder; Rakhsh reared and a cry arose.
sentences.s127-s26: When the host saw his back and mane, and the mace and Gopāl in his grasp,
sentences.s127-s27: You said that their hearts left their bodies; from terror the assembly was scattered.
sentences.s127-s28: That same king, with his famed chiefs, when they saw one by one the tokens of Rostam,
sentences.s127-s33: There was no distance between this and that kingship; good and ill, war and feast went together.
sentences.s127-s34: If you make a little cry, be one with me; I do not fear Rostam even in a brief fight.
sentences.s127-s35: Otherwise that kingship will be attained; its reach will be long, and an evil hand will be upon every side.
sentences.s127-s36: When the letter reached near them that Rostam had drawn his host onto this plain.
sentences.s127-s5: There was no man who could find a way to avoid going into battle in the manner of a brave leopard.
sentences.s127-s6: who in battle would never set an ambush, even if his heart were full of hatred.
sentences.s127-s7: If King Kavus finds you freed, you will escape from the dragon's claw and breath.
sentences.s127-s8: Otherwise, array my forces for battle; take my host upon your neck (i.e. assume responsibility for my army).
sentences.s127-s49: And another: the one who holds a friend is my greatness, and his love is my bulwark.
sentences.s127-s50: You, give the reins to your shining Rakhsh; set its ear toward the spear‑point.
sentences.s127-s51: I will leave none of them alive in the world—neither openly nor in secret.
sentences.s127-s52: A messenger quickly brought an answer; at Rostam Zāl the gold turned like smoke.
sentences.s127-s45: The messenger heard (it) and came running up to Kavus, who had gone into hiding.
sentences.s127-s46: Tahmatan's message drove everyone back; when Kavus heard it, he stood aghast.
sentences.s127-s47: He answered thus: 'My thought is not broader than this; the earth has not become wide for me.'
sentences.s127-s48: So it was while the revolving heavens lasted: that with drink there is poison, and with war there is affection.
sentences.s127-s41: That the king and the three realms were put in order — in this way they rose up from their places.
sentences.s127-s42: If I stir from my place in the fighting, because of this grudge they will not know the head from the foot.
sentences.s127-s43: One must not let this grudge bring you harm, for ill deeds come from bad people.
sentences.s127-s44: My throne will be of no use to me if misfortune befalls the sovereign's body.
sentences.s127-s29: The heavy-armed host fled to the level plains before Tahmātan.
sentences.s127-s30: When the commander sat down with an adviser, he asked the assembly for two young men.
sentences.s127-s31: He resolved that forthwith he should send them, within the moment, to Egypt and to the Berbers like a gust of the hem.
sentences.s127-s32: Each one wrote a letter and placed it within his bosom; it was written from the pain of the heart with the water of blood.
